edge: Pre-trained models have changed the landscape of Machine Learning research by shifting from training from scratch to fine-tuning paradigm.
The landscape of Machine Learning research has transitioned from training from scratch to fine-tuning paradigm due to the availability of large pre-trained models.
Two main paradigms, Reward Maximization (RM) and Distribution Matching (DM), have emerged to tackle the challenge of fine-tuning pre-trained models towards desired distributions.
Reward Maximization (RM) utilizes standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) techniques, such as Policy Gradients, to increase the reward signal gradually.
Methods like KL-control developed for Reward Maximization (RM) can also be seen as belonging to Distribution Matching (DM), showcasing theoretical connections between the two paradigms.
Reward Maximization (RM) can face challenges like high gradient variance during training.
Distribution Matching (DM) may encounter similar training difficulties as Reward Maximization (RM), such as high gradient variance.
The concept of baseline is imported into Distribution Matching (DM) methods leveraging connections between the two paradigms.
Adding a baseline proves beneficial for controllable language generation tasks, such as constraining topic, sentiment, and gender distributions, when sampled from a language model.

Reward maximization (RM) and Distribution matching (DM) are two key concepts in the context of human preferences. RM focuses on nudging pre-trained models towards certain preferences through global sequence-level rewards, while DM involves matching a distributional term inside the reward to be maximized.

Reinforcement Learning (RL) techniques can be applied under the Reward Maximization paradigm to discourage the model from producing toxic content. However, naive application of RL may lead to catastrophic forgetting of the model's original distribution.

Some studies under the Reward Maximization paradigm have incorporated KL-Control to address issues like catastrophic forgetting. This approach involves maximizing a distributional term within the reward.

There is a wide discussion on the overall effectiveness of Reward Maximization for tasks such as machine translation. This discussion involves considering the impact of RM on achieving desirable features in model outputs.
Reinforcement Learning techniques can be utilized within the Distribution Matching framework to guide model behavior towards desirable features.
The naive application of Reinforcement Learning techniques may lead to catastrophic forgetting of the model's original distribution, causing it to produce nonsensical sequences.

The overall effectiveness of Reward Maximization for tasks like machine translation has been debated in the literature. Studies have examined the impact of RM on model performance and output quality in translation tasks.

Subtracting the baseline B from the reward R(x) does not introduce bias into DPG gradient estimates. The baseline B can be the average of rewards for the policy, which can be estimated without introducing bias by averaging over samples from all the different policy parameters.
The baseline B in the DPG algorithm can be the expectation of the reward term, resulting in a remarkably simple form of baseline for DPG.
The baseline B is subtracted from the reward R(x) in equation (1), making the gradient estimates in DPG unbiased.

In practice, as shown on Figure 2, adding a baseline to KL-adaptive DPG (Algorithm 1) centers the advantage (defined as A q(x) ) around 0 leading to better performance on: convergence (section 4.3), stability on small batch sizes (section 4.4), and variance reduction (section 4.5).

In the scope of this paper, our focus is on importing to DPG simple constant baselines. The advantage is that this is a technique that is not impacted by the fact that R✓ depends on ✓: it can be applied '✓-locally'.

KL-control is considered a generalization of MaxEnt RL to informed priors in the Reinforcement Learning (RL) literature.
In certain cases, KL-control can be reduced to an entropy bonus if specific distributions are chosen.
Most practical algorithms in the MaxEnt RL family are derived from the general framework of control-as-inference, which poses control as minimizing KL from a target distribution.

MaxEnt RL family algorithms minimise KL from a target policy which changes throughout training, while DPG's target distribution p and KL-control implicit target distribution pz are deﬁned at trajectory level and ﬁxed throughout training.
AWR minimises the forward KL from an off-policy target distribution.
SMM poses exploration as learning a policy that induces a state marginal distribution that matches a target state distribution, similar to the concept of KL-control.
SMM has apparent similarity to KL-control and DPG.
SMM's target distribution is deﬁned for individual states.
In controllable language generation tasks, the target distribution is deﬁned over a complete trajectory considered as a unit.
Fine-tuning large language models has become an active area of research, with a focus on the nuanced relation between two popular ﬁne-tuning paradigms: RM and DM.
RM and DM are two popular fine-tuning paradigms. KL-control can be seen as a form of DM, indicating a relationship between the two paradigms.
DPG and PG have different goals but share similarities in the forms of gradient estimates despite different objectives. This similarity can be exploited.
Some theoretical results and algorithmic techniques from RL can be adapted to a DM framework without losing formal guarantees, suggesting a connection between DPG and RL.
An extension of DPG involves adding a baseline to reduce the variance of gradient estimates, indicating a relation between DPG and baseline.
The baseline is added to reduce the variance of gradient estimates, linking baseline to variance reduction.
One possible enhancement is further reducing variance using a learned value function, connecting baseline and learned value function.
Policy updates can be prevented from being detrimentally large by maintaining a trust region in the policy space, showing a relationship between policy updates and trust region.
The relationship between explicit EBMs in DPG and implicit EBMs in KL-control can be analyzed, suggesting a connection between explicit and implicit EBMs.
